summary: Joe Reis argues that staying current in tech requires focusing on timeless fundamentals rather than chasing trends. He advocates for identifying your 'True North' - the unchanging principles in your field - and mastering first principles deeply rather than superficially jumping between technologies.
key_points: Identify your 'True North' - focus on what doesn't change in your field
Master fundamentals from first principles rather than surface-level tutorials
Knowledge compounds slowly like interest - be patient and read widely
Avoid the Red Queen Effect of running harder just to stay in place
Reading across disciplines helps connect random ideas into original insights
money_quotes: For data professionals, I see True North as data needs to be as accurate and believable as possible, delivered as quickly and seamlessly as possible, promoting the best data-driven decisions and outcomes.

First principles mean getting back to basics. It means understanding how things work at an atomic level. This takes a ton of work, and there are no shortcuts if you want to call yourself an expert in something.
If you read Through the Looking Glass, you'll learn about the Red Queen Effect, where you run harder and harder to stay in the same place. Avoid this treadmill trap.
Knowledge compounds. But, it happens very slowly, similar to compound interest. You need to be patient.
key_insight: Instead of exhausting yourself chasing every new technology trend, invest in mastering timeless fundamentals and first principles that will remain relevant regardless of which tools come and go.
